description: Architecture with many independent nodes each with own memory connected by high-speed network; used for extreme-scale database queries
lifecycle_notes: MPP concepts continue in modern big data platforms (Teradata; AWS Redshift; Snowflake)

description: Transaction Processing Performance Council benchmark measuring OLTP throughput in transactions per minute (tpmC)
lifecycle_notes: TPC-C still actively used as of 2024; benchmark standard for OLTP database performance comparisons

description: Relational database originally developed by DEC; acquired by Oracle; optimized for VAX/VMS and Alpha environments
lifecycle_notes: Oracle Rdb continues in niche use on OpenVMS; current version Oracle Rdb 7.4

description: Sybase component delivering order-of-magnitude speedup for complex queries using columnar storage techniques
lifecycle_notes: Evolved into SAP IQ (formerly Sybase IQ); still active as SAP IQ for analytics

description: Digital's 100 MB/s cluster interconnect bus allowing in-memory data passing between cluster nodes without disk I/O
lifecycle_notes: Proprietary DEC interconnect; replaced by InfiniBand and high-speed Ethernet for cluster communications

description: High-speed networking technology enabling gigabyte-per-second bandwidth requiring fast in-memory buffering
lifecycle_notes: ATM largely replaced by Ethernet (10GbE; 100GbE) and MPLS in carrier networks

description: CPU architecture limiting addressable memory to ~2-4GB; the dominant architecture being superseded by 64-bit in 1995
lifecycle_notes: 32-bit server computing largely obsolete by mid-2000s; 32-bit client computing obsolete by 2020s

description: Tandem's fault-tolerant RDBMS for its Himalaya line designed for high-availability OLTP without LIMD support in 1995
lifecycle_notes: Continues as HPE NonStop SQL / NonStop Advanced Server; still used in financial OLTP

notes: SAP R/3 deployed in three-tier client-server architecture with presentation/application/database separation
status_note: Three-tier architecture remains standard enterprise pattern

lifecycle_note: TPC-C benchmark still used for OLTP performance measurement
name: TPC-C

notes: Industry-standard OLTP benchmark (tpmC); Digital/Oracle set world record of 11,000+ tpmC in 1995
Flagship network management application. Supported HP OpenView, Sun NetManager, and IBM NetView platforms. Windows version expected Q4 1995.
TP monitor integrated with MQSeries for transactional messaging; now Oracle Tuxedo
64-bit SPARC successor; anticipated 1996 transition caused some dampening of Sun 1995 growth
LANplex 2500 supported VLANs via port groups and MAC address groups. Cited as key user requirement.
Dominant Fortune-1000 departmental development tool alongside PowerBuilder; now VB.NET (active) and VB6 (legacy)
IBM development toolset integrating with MQSeries alongside VisualGen

description: Microsoft RAD tool mentioned as a front-end application development option for ORB-based architectures. ObjectBroker 2.5 supports a Visual Basic DLL IDL binding.
lifecycle_notes: Visual Basic evolved into VB.NET (introduced 2002 with .NET Framework). Classic VB 6.0 development ended; VB.NET remains supported but is niche relative to C#.

notes: IBM development toolset integrating with MQSeries

lifecycle_note: Superseded by modern BI tools
name: DB2 Visualizer

notes: 2 MB VRAM on Multia Intel S3 graphics card
Embedded VT terminal emulation for DEC host access; pre-configured on Multia
Supported as downloadable application environment on Multia desktop
SAP R/3 presentation layer supports Windows 3.1, Windows NT, and OS/2 GUIs simultaneously
MQSeries support described as upcoming at time of study
Base OS for Multia; provided multitasking and stability; succeeded by Windows NT 4.0 in 1996
NT 4.0 not expected until April 1996; scalability and stability issues prevented enterprise production use in 1995; expected to complement RISC/Unix

lifecycle_note: X/Open CLI largely superseded by JDBC and modern ORMs; ODBC descended from it
name: X/Open CLI

notes: X session protocol for Unix environment access; included in Multia
Multia X Server supplies X11.R6 functions; Wayland gradually replacing X11 on Linux
1980s store-and-forward email API market; predecessor context for Commercial Messaging market sizing below $100M
